segments.voice: Between February 1976 and March 1977, four children were abducted, held for days, and murdered in Oakland County, Michigan. They were returned bathed and dressed in clean clothing. The killer was never identified. The case is one of the most chilling unsolved child-murder series in American history.

The first victim was Mark Stebbins, 12, of Ferndale. He vanished on February 15, 1976 while walking home from his mother's workplace. His body was found four days later in the parking lot of an office building in Southfield. He had been suffocated.

What investigators noticed immediately was the condition of the body. Mark had been bathed. His clothing had been laundered. His fingernails had been cleaned. The killer had taken time to remove forensic evidence before disposing of the body.

The Oakland County Sheriff's Office and the Michigan State Police investigated. They had limited leads. The body had been arranged carefully. The dump location showed no signs of struggle. The killer had clearly used a vehicle.

On December 22, 1976, Jill Robinson, 12, of Royal Oak vanished after running away from home following an argument with her mother. Her body was found four days later along a roadside in Troy. She had been shot at close range with a shotgun. Like Mark, she had been bathed and dressed in clean clothing before disposal.

Investigators began connecting the cases. The pattern of abduction, days of captivity, and post-mortem cleaning suggested a single offender. Oakland County formed a task force. The Michigan State Police, Oakland County Sheriff's Office, and FBI all contributed personnel.

On January 2, 1977, Kristine Mihelich, 10, of Berkley vanished while walking to a convenience store. She was held for 19 days — the longest captivity period of any of the victims. Her body was found on January 21 in Franklin Village. She had been suffocated.

The 19-day captivity disturbed investigators. The killer had kept the child alive for almost three weeks. He had likely had access to a private space where she could be held without being heard. He had to have been someone with a stable residence and the ability to come and go without raising suspicion.

Here's where the case took its final turn. On March 16, 1977, Timothy King, 11, of Birmingham vanished after walking to a drugstore. He was held for six days. His body was found on March 22 in Livonia.

Timothy's family had pleaded publicly during his captivity. Timothy's mother had appeared on television asking for his safe return. She had specifically said she would make Timothy his favorite meal — Kentucky Fried Chicken — when he came home.

The autopsy on Timothy revealed something specific. His stomach contained Kentucky Fried Chicken eaten shortly before his death. The killer had heard the mother's televised plea and had given the boy that exact meal before killing him.

After Timothy King's death, the killings stopped. There were no more abductions fitting the pattern. The Oakland County task force continued its investigation through the late 1970s and 1980s without identifying the killer.

Several suspects were investigated over the decades. Among them was Christopher Busch, the son of a wealthy General Motors executive, who killed himself in November 1978. Busch had been investigated for unrelated child-sex offenses. His suicide came less than two years after the King killing.

Investigators searching Busch's home found a sketch on his wall that resembled Mark Stebbins. They found photographs and other items connected to child sexual offending. The Busch theory has been pursued by some retired investigators ever since.

Other suspects investigated have included John Hastings, a convicted child sex offender who died in 2004; Arch Sloan, a child molester whose vehicle showed forensic links to one of the victims; and several other regional offenders.

DNA evidence preserved from the original cases has been tested repeatedly with modern technology. In 2012, Michigan State Police announced that DNA from the Mark Stebbins case had been linked through unspecified methods to a man whose name has not been publicly released.

The Oakland County Prosecutor's Office and the Michigan State Police continue to maintain the case as open. Periodic investigative announcements have been made over the decades, including substantial public updates in 2012, 2018, and 2023.

Some retired investigators have publicly criticized the Oakland County Prosecutor's Office for what they describe as slow public disclosure of information. Others have defended the secrecy as necessary to protect ongoing investigation.

The families of the victims have remained active advocates. Mark Stebbins's mother, Ruth, lived into the 2010s before passing away without resolution. Timothy King's father has been a steady public voice for the case for decades.

The case is also studied at the FBI's Behavioral Analysis Unit as an early example of an organized child predator with elaborate post-offense behavior. The bathing and clean clothing pattern is studied as a signature.

Some researchers have argued the killings stopped because the offender died, was incarcerated, or moved out of state. Others have argued the offender continued in different jurisdictions but altered his pattern.

The Oakland County Child Killer file is one of the largest cold case files in Michigan State Police history. It includes thousands of pages of investigative reports, witness statements, and forensic analyses.

If you remember anything about Oakland County in 1976 or 1977 — particularly any unusual encounter with an adult man and a child during those months — the Michigan State Police continue to accept tips on the case.
The reward for information leading to identification of the killer remains active. The Oakland County Prosecutor's Office maintains a dedicated hotline.
Subscribe to DarkFiles and hit the bell. Next week's case goes deeper into a series of murders in 1960s Glasgow tied to a man who never gave his real name.
